g.data-narration: Welcome to Frames in OpenGL! Today we'll explore framebuffers, the foundation of modern graphics rendering.
A frame is a single image displayed on screen. In OpenGL, frames are stored in framebuffers - containers holding color, depth, and stencil data.
g.text: What is a Frame in OpenGL?

g.data-narration: Framebuffers enable powerful techniques: post-processing effects, shadow mapping, and deferred rendering all rely on rendering to off-screen buffers.
g.text: These techniques require off-screen rendering to Framebuffer Objects (FBOs)

g.data-narration: Framebuffer Objects (FBOs) are user-created off-screen rendering targets. Unlike the default framebuffer, FBOs exist in GPU memory for intermediate processing.
To create an FBO, first generate a framebuffer ID with glGenFramebuffers, then bind it with glBindFramebuffer to make it the active target.
g.text: Creating an FBO - Step by Step

g.data-narration: FBOs hold attachments - either textures or renderbuffers. Color attachments use GL_COLOR_ATTACHMENT0, depth uses GL_DEPTH_ATTACHMENT, and stencil uses GL_STENCIL_ATTACHMENT.
Renderbuffers are optimized storage for rendering but cannot be sampled by shaders. They're perfect for depth and stencil data you don't need to read back.

g.text: Fast storage • Cannot sample • Best for depth/stencil
Can sample in shaders • More flexible • Slower

g.data-narration: To render to a texture, attach it to the FBO using glFramebufferTexture2D. This allows subsequent passes to sample the rendered image.
Always check framebuffer completeness with glCheckFramebufferStatus before rendering. The FBO must meet specific criteria: matching dimensions, valid attachments, and not exceeding limits.
Double buffering uses front and back buffers to display only complete frames. The swap chain flips buffers when a frame is ready, preventing visual tearing.
Two-pass rendering first draws to an FBO texture, then samples that texture on a fullscreen quad. This enables post-processing effects like bloom or blur.
Multiple Render Targets let fragment shaders output to multiple color attachments simultaneously. Use glDrawBuffers to specify which attachments receive data.
Avoid these common mistakes: forgetting completeness checks, using renderbuffers when you need textures, mismatching attachment sizes, and not unbinding FBOs before screen rendering.
In summary: FBOs enable off-screen rendering for advanced effects. Use renderbuffers for depth/stencil, textures for sampling. Always check completeness and use double buffering for smooth animation.

g.text: FBOs provide off-screen rendering targets for advanced graphics techniques
Renderbuffers: fast but not samplable • Textures: flexible but slower
Always check framebuffer completeness before rendering
Double buffering prevents screen tearing • V-Sync controls frame rate
